metadata.notes: Wingspan standard overhaul: removed legacy sections format, added rules_citations with key rulings, validated content against official rulebook.

subtopics.title: Components & Materials
subtopics.content:
#### Board & Shared Components
- 1 double-sided game board showing 6 **charter areas** (your personal village sections) surrounding a central **commons** area
- 1 index with 36 numbered cards stored in a card archive box (you'll look these up only when the game tells you to)
- 6 charter boxes — one per player, each sealed with unique starting stickers and components
- 1 progress track board for tracking your campaign progress across all 12 games
- Crate tokens (numbered containers that unlock new content — think of them as surprise packs you earn during play)

#### Per-Player Components
- 2 wooden worker meeples in your charter's color
- 1 wooden VP marker
- 1 wooden reputation marker
- 1 wooden influence token
- Starting resource cards and coins
- A charter box containing your unique starting stickers

#### Legacy Materials
- 30+ minion tokens — these are generic workers that fill in for unoccupied charters (so all 6 areas stay active regardless of player count)
- Sealed tuck boxes and card packs that get revealed as the campaign progresses
- Sticker sheets that **permanently modify the game board**
- The board itself evolves — buildings you construct are stickered onto it and stay there for every future game

subtopics.title: First Game Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Before Your First Game
1. Punch out all tokens and sort them into labeled bags
2. Place the game board in the center of the table (use **Side 1** for the campaign)
3. Each player picks a charter (numbered 1–6) and takes the matching charter box
4. Open your charter box — apply only the stickers marked "Game 1" to your charter area on the board
5. Place 1 worker on your charter's starting building
6. Keep your second worker in reserve (you'll place it on a future turn)

#### Initial Resources
- Each player starts with a set of basic resources (coins, pumpkins, etc.) as shown on your charter card
- Place your VP marker on **10** on the score track
- Place your reputation marker on the **center** of the reputation track
- Any charter not controlled by a player gets 2 minion workers placed on its starting buildings (the Automa system keeps those areas active)

#### Checkpoint — Each Player Should Have:
- 2 workers (1 placed on your starting building, 1 in reserve)
- VP marker on 10
- Reputation marker at center
- Starting resources as shown on your charter card
- Game 1 stickers applied to your charter area

#### Important First-Game Notes
- **Do NOT open any sealed content** until the game specifically tells you to
- Read the campaign guide's "Game 1" section aloud as a group
- The index cards (numbered 1–36) are referenced throughout — only look up a card when directed

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Flow
subtopics.content:
#### Campaign Structure
- **12-game legacy campaign** — each game takes 45-75 minutes
- Between games: unlock new content, permanently add buildings, watch your village evolve based on group decisions

#### Single Game Flow
1. **Setup** — apply any new stickers, read the campaign guide entry, grab starting resources
2. **Play** — take turns placing workers on buildings to gain resources, construct new buildings, advance objectives
3. **Trigger End** — the game ends when the progress token reaches the end of the progress track
4. **Score** — tally VP from buildings, reputation, influence, guideposts, and special bonuses
5. **Between Games** — open earned crates, apply permanent changes, record scores on campaign progress track

#### Player Interaction
- **Place workers on ANY building** on the entire board, including other players' charter areas
- **Charter owner bonus** — when you visit someone else's charter, the owner gets a small bonus (typically 1 VP or 1 coin); building attractive buildings in your charter creates passive income
- **Construction restriction** — you can only construct buildings in your OWN charter, not someone else's section
- **Commons area** — neutral buildings in the center available to all
- **Indirect competition** — reputation and influence tracks create jockeying for position while building the same village

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### What You Do on Your Turn
- On your turn, do exactly **one** of three things:

#### Option 1: Place a Worker
- **Action** — put 1 worker on an empty building space anywhere on the board; immediately resolve that building's effect (gain resources, advance a track, score VP, etc.)
- **Charter ownership bonus** — if the building is in another player's charter, the owner gets a small bonus (typically 1 VP or 1 coin)

#### Option 2: Construct a Building
- **Action** — pay the resource cost on a building card in your hand, peel the matching sticker, and place it permanently on an empty construction site in **your own charter area only**
- **Benefit** — gain VP and possible bonus effects; the new building is now available for everyone on future turns

#### Option 3: Return All Workers
- **Action** — take all your workers back from the board to your supply; costs your entire turn
- Play continues clockwise until the game-end condition is met

#### The Engine Loop
- Place workers on buildings to gain resources or effects
- Spend resources to construct new buildings in your charter
- New buildings give you (and everyone else) more powerful placement options
- This cycle compounds across each game and the entire 12-game campaign

#### Bumping Workers
- **You can bump any worker on the board, even your own** — place your worker on an occupied building and the existing worker returns to its owner's supply
- **Tactical uses** — force opponents off useful buildings, or free up your own workers without spending a return turn

#### Key Concept: Charter Ownership
- **Your charter = your home base** — buildings you construct go there permanently
- **Passive income** — when other players use buildings in your charter, you benefit
- **Campaign arc** — over 12 games, a well-built charter becomes your personalized engine generating passive VP

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Resource System
- **Starting resources** — coins, pumpkins, coal, wood, and iron available from Game 1; additional types introduced through crate openings as the campaign progresses
- **Gaining resources** — place workers on production buildings or trigger building effects
- **Spending resources** — primarily to construct buildings and fulfill guidepost objectives
- **No hand limit** — store resources in your charter chest between games

#### The Reputation Track
- **Linear track** along the edge of the board; advancing earns VP at certain thresholds
- **Determines** turn order advantages and tiebreakers; some buildings and guideposts reward or require reputation advancement
- **Carries over** between games with some reset — investing early has long-term payoff

#### The Influence Track
- **Measures long-term progress** — gained primarily through constructing buildings and completing guideposts
- **Determines the campaign winner** — cumulative influence across all 12 games; most influence at end of Game 12 wins

#### Guideposts (Objectives)
- **Mission objectives** — each game has a set of guidepost cards that drive the story forward
- **Rewards** — completing a guidepost grants VP and potentially unlocks new content (crate openings, narrative events)
- **Not optional** — central to the experience with significant VP; some are competitive (first to complete gets a bonus), others available to all

#### Crate System
- **Numbered sealed cards** in the index card archive; triggered by guideposts, certain buildings, or game events
- **Can contain** — new building stickers, rule cards, resources, persona cards, story elements, and entirely new mechanics
- **Permanent additions** — once opened, new rules stay in effect for all future games; the heart of the legacy experience

#### Building Types
- **Production buildings** — gain resources when you place a worker
- **Conversion buildings** — trade one set of resources for another
- **VP buildings** — score victory points directly
- **Track buildings** — advance on the reputation or influence tracks
- **Special buildings** — unique effects introduced through crates (vary by campaign)
- **Commons buildings** — available from the start with basic effects

#### Automa (Solo and Low Player Count)
- **Manages unoccupied charters** — Automa places minion workers according to simple AI rules; charters still develop with buildings over the campaign
- **Ensures all 6 charters are active** regardless of player count — at 1-5 players, unoccupied charters run by Automa opponents

#### Persona Cards
- **Unlocked through crates** during the campaign; each gives a unique ongoing ability
- **May change or upgrade** as the campaign progresses, adding asymmetry based on which personas players claim

#### Between-Game Persistence
- Game board **retains all building stickers permanently**
- Charter chest carries accumulated cards and some resources
- Influence totals carry over as the campaign score
- New rules from opened crates remain in effect
- Some VP and reputation positions reset at the start of each new game
- Chronicle booklet accumulates story stickers tracking the narrative

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End of Game Trigger
- **Progress token reaches the end of the progress track** — advances when constructing buildings and completing guideposts
- **Finish the current round** so every player has equal turns — the game does NOT end immediately

#### In-Game Scoring
- **Constructing buildings** — each building grants VP when built (advanced buildings worth more)
- **Completing guideposts** — objective cards award VP upon completion
- **Reputation track bonuses** — reaching certain thresholds awards VP
- **Building activation bonuses** — some buildings grant VP when workers are placed on them
- **Charter ownership bonuses** — opponents using your buildings grant you VP or resources
- **End-of-game scoring** — some effects grant VP based on conditions at game end (resources held, buildings constructed, track positions)

#### End of Each Game
1. **Score end-of-game bonuses**
2. **Record each player's VP total** for the game
3. **Individual game winner** earns bonus influence
4. **Add VP** to each player's cumulative influence total on the campaign tracker
5. **Resolve story/Chronicle events** triggered by the game's conclusion
6. **Prepare board state** for the next game (some elements reset, board and buildings persist)

#### Campaign Victory (After Game 12)
- **Highest cumulative influence** across all 12 games wins the campaign
- **Influence** = total VP across all games + bonus influence from game wins and special achievements
- **Ties are friendly** — tied players share the victory; no tiebreaker

#### Post-Campaign (Recharge Pack)
- **Recharge Pack** (sold separately) — play a second campaign on the reverse side of the board
- **Standalone mode** — your completed campaign board becomes a replayable worker placement game with no legacy elements; a permanent custom game unique to your group

#### Typical Scoring
- **Early campaign** (Games 1-4) — 30-50 VP per game, fewer buildings and options
- **Mid campaign** (Games 5-8) — 50-80 VP per game as engines develop
- **Late campaign** (Games 9-12) — 80-120+ VP per game with fully developed charters
- **Campaign totals** — 700-1000+ cumulative influence depending on consistency

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Opening Priorities
- **Build in your own charter first** — ownership bonuses trigger whenever anyone uses your buildings; early construction creates a passive VP engine that compounds over the entire campaign
- **Diversify resource production** — being locked out of a key resource (e.g., iron for construction) stalls your plans
- **Complete guideposts quickly** — unlock new content through crates; first player to complete often gets a bonus reward
- **Use other players' buildings strategically** — sometimes an opponent's production building offers exactly what you need; just remember they gain a bonus when you visit
- **Manage workers carefully** — with only 2 workers, every placement matters; don't return too early or wait too long
- **Invest in reputation early** — VP thresholds and tiebreakers; falling behind is hard to recover from later

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- **Ignoring charter development** — spending all time using others' buildings instead of constructing your own; your charter is your long-term engine
- **Hoarding resources** — resources are meant for construction; sitting on piles while opponents build puts you behind
- **Neglecting guideposts** — they drive the story, unlock mechanics, and award significant VP; not optional
- **Building randomly** — synergistic buildings outperform unrelated ones; aim for a coherent resource-to-VP conversion chain
- **Forgetting the campaign arc** — decisions in Game 1 affect Game 12; a weaker building that unlocks future combos beats a short-term VP grab
- **Not reading crate content carefully** — new rules from crates are permanent; missing a key rule change costs you for multiple games

#### Campaign-Level Strategy
- **Consistency wins campaigns** — placing in the top half consistently accumulates more influence than winning 3 games and losing 9
- **Adapt to new mechanics** — each crate opening changes the game; integrating new rules quickly gives an edge over those stuck in old patterns
- **Watch influence totals** — if behind, take more risks; if ahead, play conservatively to protect your lead
- **Develop a charter identity** — lean into a clear strategic focus (resource powerhouse, reputation specialist, conversion engine) rather than trying to do everything

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Charterstone

**Setup**: Open your charter box, apply Game 1 stickers to your board area. Place 1 worker on your starting building, keep the second in reserve. VP marker on 10, reputation at center, grab starting resources.

#### Checkpoint — You Should Have:
- 1 worker on your starting building, 1 in reserve
- VP on 10, reputation at center
- Starting resources in hand

**Early Turns (1-5)**
- Use your starting building to collect basic resources (pumpkins, coins, wood)
- Visit commons buildings in the center for additional actions
- Start saving resources toward your first construction — check building cards in hand for what you can afford

**Mid-Game (6-12)**
- Construct your first building — pay its resource cost, peel the sticker onto an empty construction site in your charter (permanent, choose thoughtfully)
- Explore other players' charters when they offer what you need (you can visit anyone's buildings but only **construct** in your own)
- Work toward guideposts for bonus VP and crate unlocks
- **Remember**: bump workers when a building you need is occupied — placing on an occupied building sends that worker home

**Late Game (13-end)**
- Focus on scoring: construct high-VP buildings, complete guideposts, push up the reputation track
- Convert all resources to VP before game ends — don't hoard
- Watch the progress track: when it fills, the round finishes and the game is over

**After Game 1**
- Open earned crates and follow instructions inside (read aloud so everyone knows new rules)
- Record scores on the campaign progress track
- Non-winners fill in additional capacity bubbles on their charter chest (catch-up bonus for Game 2)
- After Game 1, keep ALL coins, resources, and advancement cards for Game 2 (capacity limits start after Game 2)
- New rules and buildings get introduced — the game gets richer each session

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Campaign-Level Thinking
- **Building placement is permanent** — every building stays for all 12 games; think about future engine value, not just the current game
- **Prioritize broadly useful effects** — resource generation, card draw, track advancement stay relevant as the game evolves; a pumpkin building might help Game 3 but sit unused in Games 8-12

#### Charter Territory Control
- **Fill your charter with strong, attractive buildings** — every building gives you a bonus when others visit; 5 broadly useful buildings = opponents feeding you resources every game
- **Prioritize buildings others want to use** — production and conversion buildings over narrow effects only you'd care about

#### Reputation vs. Influence
- **Reputation** — end-game VP and ongoing bonuses; high reputation scores more from guideposts and threshold rewards
- **Influence** — determines overall campaign winner and breaks ties
- **Balance** — lean toward reputation early (VP compounding matters most) and toward influence in final games

#### Crate Optimization
- **Earning crates earlier** means more games to exploit that content
- **Prioritize guidepost completion over raw VP** — new mechanics generate far more VP across future games than a few points now

#### Adaptation
- **The game evolves substantially** across 12 sessions; strategies from Games 1-4 may become obsolete by Game 8
- **Stay flexible** — read new cards carefully, shift your engine when something better appears
- **Players who adapt fastest** to each wave of new content gain a lasting edge

key_rulings.topic: Workers can be placed on any building
key_rulings.text: On your turn, you may place a worker on any unoccupied building space on the entire board — including buildings in other players' charter areas and the commons.
When another player places a worker on a building in your charter, you receive a small bonus (typically 1 VP or 1 coin). This creates a passive income stream from buildings you construct.
Once a building sticker is placed on the game board, it remains there permanently for all future games in the campaign. Building placement decisions are irreversible.

key_rulings.topic: Returning workers costs a full turn
key_rulings.text: To return all your workers from the board to your supply, you must spend your entire turn. You cannot place a worker and return workers on the same turn.
When a crate is opened, all new rules, resources, and mechanics it introduces are permanent additions to the game. They remain in effect for all subsequent games in the campaign.

key_rulings.topic: Progress track ends the game — finish the round
key_rulings.text: When the progress token advances to the last space, finish the current round so every player has had an equal number of turns. The game does NOT end immediately when the token hits the final space.

The overall campaign winner is the player with the highest cumulative influence across all 12 games. Influence equals total VP earned across all games plus bonus influence from winning individual games.

You can bump any regular worker on the board, even your own. When you place your worker on an occupied building, the existing worker (yours or an opponent's) is returned to its owner's supply. This is a key strategic mechanism.

Ties are consistently 'friendly' in Charterstone. Multiple players can win the game, have the most reputation, or achieve a guidepost simultaneously. There is no tiebreaker — tied players all count as sharing that position.

key_rulings.topic: Crate ownership — your crate regardless of source
key_rulings.text: If you unlock a crate on a constructed building card, no matter how you obtained that card or the content on that card, it is YOUR crate. If the Index Guide header tells you to gain cards from it, YOU gain those cards.

After Game 1, players keep ALL coins, resources, and advancement cards for Game 2. Starting from the end of Game 2 onward, each player discards components down to the Capacity of their Charter Chest. Non-winners fill in additional capacity bubbles as a catch-up mechanism.

key_rulings.topic: Buildings can only be constructed in your charter
key_rulings.text: There is no connection between any building cards and any specific charter. If you have a building card, you can construct it. However, the only eligible location to construct a building is in your own charter's open construction sites.
